title: Nvidia GB200 Forces Chassis Sector Pivot to Liquid Cooling
text:
- Infrastructure Challenge: Nvidia’s GB200 platform requires 120kW per rack power density, forcing server chassis manufacturers to abandon air-cooled designs.
- Deployment Compromise: Datacenters are adopting the split NVL36x2 configuration with 66kW per rack despite higher operating costs and complexity.
- Copper Over Optics: Nvidia chose 5,184 copper cables for NVL72 interconnect to avoid the $2.2M per rack cost of optical transceivers.
- Market Impact: Chenbro’s 2025 revenue exceeded NT$20 billion as AI server demand drove transition to integrated liquid cooling systems.
- Future Direction: The Rubin platform will achieve 100% liquid cooling coverage with independent power racks and fanless modular design.
Server chassis manufacturers are pivoting from decades of air-cooled designs as Nvidia’s GB200 systems cross the 120kW power density threshold that existing datacenter infrastructure cannot support.
The GB200 platform requires triple H100’s 40kW density, forcing the sector to adopt integrated liquid cooling systems. At CES 2026, Nvidia presented the Rubin platform with independent POWER RACK design separating power modules from compute, signaling complete sector transformation.
NVL36x2 Becomes Default Despite Economics
This thermal barrier creates an immediate deployment dilemma. Existing datacenter infrastructure cannot support 120kW per rack density even with direct-to-chip liquid cooling, forcing adoption of GB200 NVL36x2 despite its economic penalties, which is estimated to cost approximately $1.8 million per rack.
NVL36x2 splits the system into two racks side by side with 66kW per rack (132kW total). It uses ~10kW more power than NVL72 due to 36 NVSwitch5 ASICs compared to NVL72’s 18. Meanwhile, NVL36x2 copper cabling costs more than double versus NVL72 due to additional switches, cables, and connectors bridging the two racks.
NVL72 offers superior power efficiency and simpler cabling but remains physically undeployable in existing facilities. Datacenters face a choice between expensive infrastructure upgrades to accommodate 120kW racks or accepting NVL36x2’s higher operating costs and cable management complexity. The thermal threshold represents a practical limit where traditional datacenter architecture cannot scale, forcing infrastructure operators to choose between costly retrofits and compromised configurations.
Copper Cables Win Over $2.2M Optics
While the thermal solution dictated rack configuration, interconnect decisions shaped cost structure. Nvidia chose 5,184 copper cables for NVL72 instead of optical transceivers as a cheaper, less power-hungry, and more reliable option.
Using optical transceivers would have required 648 twin port 1.6T transceivers consuming approximately 30W each, adding 19.4kW per NVL72 rack. At $850 per transceiver, optical NVLink would cost $550,800 per rack in transceivers alone, or $2.2M per rack at Nvidia’s 75% gross margin.
Each GB200 NVL72 rack consists of 18 1U compute trays and 9 NVSwitch trays, with each compute tray containing 2 Bianca boards. Each Bianca board contains 1 Grace CPU and 2 Blackwell GPUs, changing the CPU:GPU ratio to 1:2 compared to GH200’s 1:1 ratio.
To simplify thermal design, GB200 uses the same cold plate to cool CPUs, GPUs, and ConnectX-7/8 NICs simultaneously via mezzanine board design.
However, the liquid cooling implementation proved less comprehensive than initially assumed. Global Semi Research noted a common misconception about GB200’s thermal solution:
“There’s a lesser-known fact about the GB200’s liquid cooling worth mentioning. I originally thought that all GB200 servers (Compute trays and switch trays) were liquid-cooled, but this is not the case.”
Global Semi Research, Article author (via Global Semi Research)
GB200 liquid cooling coverage was only 70%-80%, with the NVL36 x 2 configuration remaining predominantly air-cooled. Key components such as power supplies still rely on air cooling.
The hybrid approach creates complexity for chassis manufacturers who must design thermal systems supporting both liquid and air cooling simultaneously. This adds cost and engineering requirements compared to uniform thermal solutions.
Amphenol and Connector Suppliers Capture Cable Windfall
The copper cable decision created clear winners in the supply chain. Nvidia chose Amphenol’s Ultrapass Paladin backplane Interconnect System as the primary initial source for NVLink backplane interconnect.
The bulk of NVLink cable cost comes from termination and connectors rather than cables themselves, as connectors must prevent crosstalk between differential pairs. The copper cable decision creates winners among connector manufacturers while optical transceiver vendors miss the GB200 cycle entirely.
For optics, Nvidia is expanding its supply chain to include Eoptolink alongside Fabrinet and Innolight, with Eoptolink focusing on 800G LPO and 1.6T with DSP. Broadcom enters the DSP market for GB200 after Marvell had 100% share on H100 generation, with Innolight and Eoptolink adding Broadcom Digital Signal Processors (DSPs) in volume.
The shift from NVLink copper to datacenter networking optics creates new opportunities for optical component suppliers, though at lower volumes than NVLink would have provided. The NVLink copper decision creates a bifurcated supplier ecosystem where connector manufacturers capture immediate GB200 revenue while optical vendors compete for datacenter networking opportunities at reduced scale.
Chenbro Revenue Surge Signals Sector Transformation
These component-level decisions translated into measurable market impact. AI server demand pushed revenue of Server chassis maker Chenbro Micom above NT$20 billion.
The company’s December 2025 revenue set monthly records as hyperscalers transitioned from air-cooled mechanical assemblies to integrated liquid cooling systems. Traditional chassis design centered on airflow management and mechanical rigidity, but suppliers now must master thermal engineering and fluid dynamics to remain competitive in AI server deployments. Analysis from Swiss private bank Lombard Odier identified advanced cooling infrastructure as “a defining competitive advantage” for 2026.
Nvidia’s Rubin platform moves power modules out of integrated cabinet to create independent POWER RACK, addressing GB200’s power delivery limitations. Rubin achieves 100% liquid cooling coverage for the first time, using cable-free, fanless modular design that reduces cabinet air volume requirements by 80% compared to GB300.
For chassis manufacturers, Rubin’s design eliminates traditional air cooling expertise entirely, requiring complete engineering capability transformation from mechanical assembly to fluid dynamics mastery.
GB300 Leakage Risk Drove Rubin Design Reversal
Rubin’s clean-sheet approach emerged from GB300’s engineering missteps. GB200 adopted integrated liquid cold plate with two large cold plates in each Compute tray.
However, GB300 shifted to independent small cold plates with 6 small cold plates per Compute Tray (two B300 GPUs and Grace CPU each had dedicated small cold plates). GB300’s small cold plate design led to surge in number of adapters and minifolds, increasing risk of liquid leakage.
This proliferation of connection points proved untenable at scale. Rubin returns to large cold plate design per Compute tray with microchannel technology using nanoscale flow channels fabricated via laser welding.
The large plate approach reduces connection points while microchannel technology maintains fine-grained thermal control that GB300 sought with separate cold plates. Rubin’s 100% liquid cooling coverage and cable-free design eliminate both air cooling infrastructure and copper cable complexity simultaneously. Cabinet air volume requirements drop 80% compared to GB300.
GB300’s design iteration revealed the inherent complexity of balancing granular thermal control against reliability concerns, as each additional connection point introduces potential failure modes in high-density deployments. Rubin’s microchannel approach solves this by maintaining thermal precision through internal channel design rather than external plumbing proliferation.
Hyperscalers Customize Network Infrastructure
While Nvidia defined thermal architecture, deployment realities diverged at the network layer. Customers will predominantly opt for 200G of frontend bandwidth instead of reference design’s two Bluefield-3 NICs providing 800Gb/s total, saving $3.5k per system in transceiver costs.
Oracle is the only major customer expected to use Bluefield-3 as frontend NIC, while Amazon, Google, and Microsoft will use their existing custom frontend NICs.
Amazon will use custom backend 400G NIC requiring adapter mezzanine board that splits Mirror Mezz connectors into 8 MCIO PCIe connectors. Google switched back to Nvidia ConnectX-8 NICs for GB200 after historically using custom backend NICs from Intel.
Each hyperscaler’s network topology decisions cascade through chassis design, as physical connector placement and thermal management must accommodate custom mezzanine boards with varying power dissipation characteristics.
Reference designs provide starting points, but hyperscaler production deployments diverge based on existing network infrastructure and workload profiles. Chassis manufacturers must support multiple configuration variants, complicating inventory management and production planning while adapting cooling systems to handle different thermal profiles across custom NIC implementations.
B200 x86 Variant and Rubin Trajectory
Near-term iterations will test these design principles further. B200 NVL72 and NVL36x2 form factor with x86 CPUs (Miranda) will arrive in Q2 2025, replacing Grace CPUs.
Transition from ConnectX-7 to ConnectX-8 will drive shift from 400G SR4 optical transceivers to 800G DR4 transceivers using single mode 200G EML. Rubin’s design characteristics – cable-free, fanless modular design that reduces cabinet air volume requirements by 80% compared to GB300 – demonstrate the trajectory toward higher power density systems.
Rubin GPU power consumption is 2.3kW, pushing per-rack power densities beyond what GB200 established. Chassis manufacturers face continuous design iteration as each GPU generation increases thermal output faster than incremental cooling improvements can address. The sector’s transformation from mechanical assembly to thermal engineering expertise will define competitive positioning through Rubin’s deployment cycle and beyond.
